prompt: In the NASM OPT model, which phase emphasizes improving muscular endurance and stabilizing the kinetic chain?

options.text: Phase 1: Stabilization Endurance
Phase 3: Muscular Development (Hypertrophy)
Phase 4: Maximal Strength
Phase 5: Power only

explanation: Phase 1 focuses on endurance, neuromuscular efficiency, and core/joint stability before heavy loading.

distractorExplanations.b: Hypertrophy emphasizes muscle size with moderate-to-heavy loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Maximal strength targets peak force production.
distractorExplanations.d: Power blends strength and speed after foundational phases.

prompt: Which muscle is the primary agonist for hip extension?

options.text: Iliacus
Gluteus maximus
Rectus femoris (isolated hip flexion role)
Anterior tibialis

explanation: Gluteus maximus is the primary hip extensor; iliopsoas/rectus femoris flex the hip; tibialis anterior dorsiflexes.

distractorExplanations.c: Rectus femoris flexes the hip and extends the knee.
distractorExplanations.d: Tibialis anterior acts at the ankle.

prompt: What plane of motion divides the body into left and right halves?

options.text: Frontal (coronal) plane
Transverse plane
Sagittal plane
Scapular plane only

explanation: Sagittal = left/right; frontal = anterior/posterior; transverse = superior/inferior.

distractorExplanations.b: Transverse separates upper and lower.
distractorExplanations.d: Scapular plane is a functional arm-elevation plane, not a cardinal body plane.

prompt: Which connective tissue attaches muscle to bone?

options.text: Ligament
Bursa
Meniscus only
Tendon

explanation: Tendons connect muscle to bone; ligaments connect bone to bone; bursae reduce friction.

distractorExplanations.b: Bursae are fluid sacs, not attachments.
distractorExplanations.c: Menisci are fibrocartilage cushions in some joints.

prompt: Type I muscle fibers are best described as:

options.text: Slow-twitch, fatigue-resistant fibers used heavily in endurance and postural work
Fast-twitch fibers specialized only for one-rep max lifts
Non-contractile connective tissue
Fibers that cannot use oxygen

explanation: Type I fibers are oxidative and fatigue-resistant; Type II are faster and more fatigable.

distractorExplanations.b: Maximal power/force relies more on Type II fibers.
distractorExplanations.c: Muscle fibers are contractile tissue.
distractorExplanations.d: Type I fibers are highly oxidative.

prompt: Which structure is the functional unit of a skeletal muscle fiber?

options.text: Nephron
Sarcomere
Osteon
Alveolus

explanation: Sarcomeres (actin/myosin arrangement between Z-lines) produce force; other units belong to kidney, bone, and lung.

distractorExplanations.c: Osteons are bone structural units.
distractorExplanations.d: Alveoli are gas-exchange units in lungs.

prompt: In OPT Phase 5 (Power), training typically pairs:

options.text: Only long slow distance cardio with no resistance
Isometric holds exclusively at end range
A heavy strength exercise with a lighter, high-velocity power exercise for the same movement pattern
Complete rest from all lower-body work for 8 weeks

explanation: Power phases use complex/contrast pairing of strength + explosive work after foundations are built.

distractorExplanations.b: Isometrics alone do not develop rate of force development.
distractorExplanations.d: Complete rest is not a power protocol.

prompt: Which muscle concentrically produces scapular retraction?

options.text: Serratus anterior (primary protraction)
Pectoralis minor only
Upper trapezius elevation only
Middle trapezius / rhomboids

explanation: Middle trapezius and rhomboids retract the scapula; serratus anterior protracts/upwardly rotates.

distractorExplanations.b: Pectoralis minor contributes to protraction/depression, not primary retraction.
distractorExplanations.c: Upper trapezius mainly elevates/upwardly rotates.

prompt: What is the primary role of the transverse abdominis in core training?

options.text: Provide deep local stabilization by increasing intra-abdominal pressure and bracing the lumbar spine
Serve as the primary prime mover for heavy spinal extension under load
Replace the need for any hip musculature
Act only as a respiratory muscle with no spinal role

explanation: TrA is a local stabilizer that supports segmental control; global movers create larger trunk motions.

distractorExplanations.b: Erector spinae are major extensors.
distractorExplanations.c: Hip musculature remains essential for force transfer.
distractorExplanations.d: TrA contributes to both bracing and respiratory timing.

prompt: Which joint action occurs in the frontal plane at the shoulder?

options.text: Flexion / extension only
Abduction / adduction
Internal / external rotation only
Pronation / supination of the forearm only

explanation: Shoulder abduction/adduction occur in the frontal plane; flexion/extension are sagittal; rotation is transverse.

distractorExplanations.c: Rotation occurs primarily in the transverse plane.
distractorExplanations.d: Pronation/supination occur at the radioulnar joints.

prompt: According to the length–tension relationship, a muscle produces optimal force when:

options.text: It is placed in extreme passive insufficiency with no overlap possible
It is completely slack with sarcomeres fully bunched always
Actin and myosin have an optimal overlap (near resting length)
The nervous system is turned off

explanation: Force peaks near optimal sarcomere length; too short or too long reduces cross-bridge capacity.

distractorExplanations.a: Extreme stretch reduces overlap and force.
distractorExplanations.b: Extreme shortening also reduces force capacity.
distractorExplanations.d: Neural drive is required for voluntary force.

prompt: Which energy system supplies the majority of ATP for a maximal effort lasting about 8–12 seconds?

options.text: Oxidative system as the sole source
Only beta-oxidation of fats
Protein deamination exclusively
ATP–PC (phosphagen) system

explanation: Very short maximal efforts rely primarily on phosphagen stores; oxidative metabolism dominates longer work.

distractorExplanations.b: Fat oxidation is too slow for short maximal power.
distractorExplanations.c: Protein is not the primary fuel for brief maximal efforts.

prompt: The agonist for a movement is best defined as the muscle that:

options.text: Is the primary mover producing the desired joint action
Only opposes the movement (antagonist role)
Only stabilizes without producing force
Is always a Type I fiber exclusively

explanation: Agonists produce the main action; antagonists oppose; synergists assist; stabilizers control unwanted motion.

distractorExplanations.b: That describes the antagonist.
distractorExplanations.c: Stabilizers control, but agonists create the primary action.
distractorExplanations.d: Fiber type is independent of agonist/antagonist role.

prompt: In NASM terminology, which subsystem includes the deep longitudinal subsystem (DLS) concept?

options.text: Only the integumentary system
Global muscular subsystems that transfer force across the kinetic chain
Only the digestive tract musculature
Cartilage-only connective tissue with no muscle

explanation: Local and global muscular subsystems (including DLS, POS, AOS, LS) describe force transfer and stabilization roles.

distractorExplanations.a: Skin is not a muscular subsystem.
distractorExplanations.c: GI smooth muscle is unrelated to OPT subsystems.
distractorExplanations.d: Subsystem models include muscular linkages.

prompt: Which bone landmark is the insertion commonly associated with the biceps brachii?

options.text: Greater trochanter of the femur
Medial malleolus
Radial tuberosity
Olecranon process only as the sole biceps insertion

explanation: Biceps brachii inserts primarily on the radial tuberosity and bicipital aponeurosis.

distractorExplanations.a: Greater trochanter is a hip landmark.
distractorExplanations.b: Medial malleolus is distal tibia.
distractorExplanations.d: Olecranon is the triceps insertion region.

prompt: During an overhead squat assessment, excessive forward lean most commonly indicates overactivity of which muscles?

options.text: Hip flexor complex and/or soleus (with possible underactivity of gluteus maximus/erectors depending on findings)
Only the deep neck flexors with no lower-body involvement
Only the wrist extensors
Only the tibialis anterior as the sole cause always

explanation: Forward lean often relates to tight calves/hip flexors and weak posterior chain; interpret the whole kinetic chain.

distractorExplanations.b: Cervical findings alone do not explain trunk lean.
distractorExplanations.c: Wrist muscles do not drive squat trunk angle.
distractorExplanations.d: Tibialis anterior tightness is not the classic forward-lean driver.

prompt: Feet turning out in the overhead squat assessment most commonly suggests overactivity of:

options.text: Only pectoralis major
Soleus / lateral gastrocnemius and/or biceps femoris (short head) with underactive medial gastroc/medial hamstrings/gracilis/sartorius/popliteus as applicable
Only upper trapezius without lower extremity involvement
Only the masseter

explanation: Foot/ankle compensation patterns often involve lateral calf and biceps femoris overactivity.

distractorExplanations.a: Chest muscles do not turn the feet out.
distractorExplanations.c: Upper trap relates more to shoulder elevation.
distractorExplanations.d: Jaw muscles are unrelated.

prompt: What does a resting heart rate assessment primarily help a trainer establish?

options.text: Exact 1RM squat without testing
Body fat percentage by itself
A baseline cardiorespiratory status and training intensity reference (with other metrics)
Bone mineral density

explanation: RHR is a simple baseline; programming still needs goals, history, and other assessments.

distractorExplanations.a: Strength must be tested or estimated separately.
distractorExplanations.b: Body composition needs other methods.
distractorExplanations.d: BMD requires medical imaging.

prompt: Which assessment is most appropriate as a dynamic postural observation of the kinetic chain under load?

options.text: Skinfold only
Sit-and-reach only
Resting blood pressure only
Overhead squat assessment

explanation: OHSA observes movement compensations; skinfolds/flexibility/BP provide other data types.

distractorExplanations.b: Sit-and-reach is flexibility-focused.
distractorExplanations.c: BP is a vital sign, not a movement screen.

prompt: Knee valgus (knees cave in) during a squat commonly involves underactivity of:

options.text: Gluteus medius / maximus (among other contributors)
Only wrist flexors
Only deep neck flexors as the sole cause
Only finger extensors

explanation: Knee valgus often pairs with weak hip abductors/external rotators and overactive adductors/TFL patterns.

distractorExplanations.b: Wrist flexors do not control knee frontal-plane motion.
distractorExplanations.c: Neck flexors are not the primary knee-valgus drivers.
distractorExplanations.d: Finger muscles are irrelevant here.

prompt: A PAR-Q+ is primarily used to:

options.text: Measure VO2max in a laboratory
Screen for health risks and determine whether medical clearance or modified programming is needed before exercise
Prescribe medications
Replace informed consent entirely in all jurisdictions

explanation: Preparticipation screening tools identify red flags; they do not replace clinical diagnosis or all legal forms.

distractorExplanations.c: Trainers do not prescribe drugs.
distractorExplanations.d: Consent/waiver processes remain separate requirements.

prompt: Which circumference measurement site is commonly used when estimating body composition with circumference formulas?

options.text: Only the earlobe
Only the toenail bed
Waist (among other sites depending on protocol)
Only the scalp hairline

explanation: Waist, hip, neck, and limb sites appear in various circumference protocols; choose the protocol’s sites.

distractorExplanations.a: Earlobe is not a standard girth site for body-comp formulas.
distractorExplanations.b: Toenails are not circumference sites.
distractorExplanations.d: Hairline is not used for girth equations.

prompt: In a pushing assessment, shoulders elevating commonly indicates overactivity of:

options.text: Only gluteus maximus
Only gastrocnemius
Only tibialis posterior
Upper trapezius / levator scapulae (with underactive mid/lower trapezius as applicable)

explanation: Scapular elevation compensations often involve upper trap/levator overactivity.

distractorExplanations.b: Calves act at the ankle.
distractorExplanations.c: Tibialis posterior supports the medial arch.

prompt: What is a key reason to assess blood pressure before vigorous exercise programming?

options.text: Uncontrolled hypertension is a risk factor that may require medical clearance and intensity modification
BP has no relevance to exercise safety
High BP always means the client should sprint intervals that day
Low readings alone diagnose all cardiac disease

explanation: Elevated BP can contraindicate or modify exercise; trainers refer as indicated.

distractorExplanations.b: BP is a core preparticipation vital.
distractorExplanations.c: High BP warrants caution, not automatic HIIT.
distractorExplanations.d: Diagnosis belongs to licensed clinicians.

prompt: The Rockport Walk Test is primarily used to estimate:

options.text: Maximal bench press 1RM
Cardiorespiratory fitness (VO2-related estimate) from a timed 1-mile walk and heart rate
Flexibility of the hamstrings only
Bone density

explanation: Field walk tests estimate aerobic fitness without a full lab VO2 protocol.

distractorExplanations.c: Flexibility needs ROM tests.
distractorExplanations.d: BMD needs medical testing.

prompt: Lower crossed syndrome is classically associated with:

options.text: Tight deep neck flexors and weak upper trapezius only
Only weak finger flexors
Tight hip flexors and erector spinae with weak gluteus maximus and abdominals
Perfectly balanced anterior and posterior chains always

explanation: Janda’s lower crossed pattern: anterior pelvic tilt with the classic tight/weak pairings.

distractorExplanations.a: That pairing is closer to upper crossed ideas reversed/incorrect.
distractorExplanations.b: Hand muscles are not the defining pattern.
distractorExplanations.d: The syndrome is defined by imbalance.

prompt: When a client reports sharp joint pain during an assessment, the trainer should:

options.text: Increase load to 'push through' sharp pain
Ignore pain if the client is motivated
Diagnose a ligament tear on the spot as a definitive medical diagnosis
Stop the painful movement and refer out / seek medical clearance as appropriate

explanation: Sharp pain is a stop-and-refer cue; trainers do not diagnose injuries.

distractorExplanations.a: Sharp pain is not trained through.
distractorExplanations.b: Motivation does not override safety.
distractorExplanations.c: Diagnosis is outside CPT scope.

prompt: Davies test is primarily designed to assess:

options.text: Upper-body agility and stabilization (reactive hand touches in plank)
Maximal deadlift strength
Resting metabolic rate
Visual acuity

explanation: Davies test challenges shoulder stability/agility in a plank position.

distractorExplanations.b: Max strength needs 1RM or estimated strength tests.
distractorExplanations.c: RMR needs metabolic measurement.
distractorExplanations.d: Vision is not a CPT motor test.

prompt: Which finding during single-leg squat assessment is a common compensation?

options.text: Perfect bilateral symmetry with no possible fault ever
Knee valgus / hip drop / trunk lean (depending on observation)
Only ear asymmetry
Only fingernail length changes

explanation: SLS reveals frontal-plane control deficits at hip/knee and trunk strategies.

distractorExplanations.c: Ears are not the focus of SLS.
distractorExplanations.d: Nails are irrelevant.

prompt: BMI is best described as:

options.text: A direct gold-standard measure of visceral fat mass
A strength index equal to 1RM
A height–weight ratio that estimates weight category risk but does not measure body fat directly
A flexibility score

explanation: BMI screens weight status; athletes and older adults may be misclassified versus true composition.

distractorExplanations.b: BMI is not a strength metric.
distractorExplanations.d: Flexibility uses ROM tests.

prompt: For a new client in Stabilization Endurance, which acute variable set is most typical?

options.text: 12–20 reps, 1–3 sets, slow tempos, shorter rest, lower intensity
1–5 reps at near-maximal loads with long rest only
Only 100-rep sets with no rest ever
Plyometric depth jumps on day one without preparation

explanation: Phase 1 uses higher reps, controlled tempos, and stability emphasis before heavy/power work.

distractorExplanations.b: Low-rep maximal loading fits strength phases later.
distractorExplanations.c: Extreme fatigue sets are not the standard Phase 1 template.
distractorExplanations.d: High-intensity plyometrics require preparation.

prompt: Which exercise progression is most appropriate for core in early stabilization training?

options.text: Maximal sit-up contests on day one
Floor bridges / dead bugs / bird dogs before loaded spinal flexion machines
Heavy standing Russian twists with end-range spinal rotation under fatigue for beginners
Loaded good mornings to failure for all new clients

explanation: Start with controlled anti-extension/anti-rotation stability before aggressive loaded trunk work.

distractorExplanations.a: High-volume sit-ups are not ideal first progressions.
distractorExplanations.c: Aggressive loaded rotation is advanced.
distractorExplanations.d: Loaded spinal flexion/hinge to failure is inappropriate for novices.

prompt: SAQ training primarily develops:

options.text: Only maximal hypertrophy
Only static flexibility
Speed, agility, and quickness (reaction and change of direction)
Only bone mineral density as the sole outcome

explanation: SAQ drills improve acceleration, COD, and reactive ability alongside other programming.

distractorExplanations.b: Flexibility is ROM-focused.
distractorExplanations.d: BMD may improve with loading but is not SAQ’s primary aim.

prompt: Which modality is most appropriate for correcting an overhead squat arms-fall-forward compensation in a corrective sequence?

options.text: Only foam roll the calves and ignore the upper body
Only train heavy bench press
Only stretch the plantar fascia
Inhibit/lengthen latissimus dorsi and pectorals; activate/integrate mid/lower trapezius and rhomboids as indicated

explanation: Arms-fall-forward often involves tight lats/pecs and weak scapular retractors/depressors.

distractorExplanations.a: Calves may matter for other faults, not this one primarily.
distractorExplanations.b: Heavy pressing can worsen the pattern.
distractorExplanations.c: Plantar fascia is unrelated.

prompt: In muscular development (hypertrophy-focused) programming, a common repetition range is:

options.text: Approximately 6–12 reps at moderate-to-challenging loads
Only 30–40 reps with empty bar always
Only singles at 100% 1RM every set
Zero resistance forever

explanation: Hypertrophy commonly uses mid-range reps with sufficient tension and volume.

distractorExplanations.b: Very high reps shift toward endurance.
distractorExplanations.c: Constant maximal singles fit peak strength peaking, not typical hypertrophy blocks.
distractorExplanations.d: Progressive overload requires resistance.

prompt: What is the primary purpose of a dynamic warm-up before resistance training?

options.text: Completely exhaust the client before the session
Increase tissue temperature, nervous system readiness, and movement-specific ROM
Replace all cool-down needs forever
Diagnose disease

explanation: Warm-ups prepare tissues and motor patterns; they should not create excessive fatigue.

distractorExplanations.c: Cool-down is still useful.
distractorExplanations.d: Diagnosis is clinical.

prompt: Plyometric training should generally be introduced when the client can:

options.text: Barely balance on two feet with pain
Skip all foundational phases regardless of ability
Demonstrate adequate strength, landing mechanics, and joint stability for the chosen drill
Only if they refuse resistance training forever

explanation: Impact drills require landing control and strength baselines to manage injury risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Pain and poor balance are contraindications to progress.
distractorExplanations.b: Foundations matter for safety.
distractorExplanations.d: Resistance training complements plyometrics.

prompt: Which rest interval is most typical between heavy maximal strength sets?

options.text: 0–10 seconds only always
24 hours between every single set
No rest by definition of strength
About 3–5 minutes (longer recovery for neural/ATP–PC restoration)

explanation: High-load strength work needs longer rest than endurance circuits.

distractorExplanations.a: Very short rests limit load quality.
distractorExplanations.b: Inter-set rest is minutes, not a day.
distractorExplanations.c: Strength training uses planned rest.

prompt: An appropriate beginner progression for a squat pattern is:

options.text: Bodyweight squat or assisted squat to box → goblet squat → barbell variations as technique allows
Maximal back squat on session one for all clients
Pistol squat to failure on day one for every sedentary adult
Skip squats and only shrug maximally

explanation: Progress complexity and load after pattern competency.

distractorExplanations.b: Max loading without skill is unsafe.
distractorExplanations.c: Unilateral advanced variants are not universal day-one drills.
distractorExplanations.d: Shrugs do not train the squat pattern.

prompt: Which statement about progressive overload is correct?

options.text: Clients should never change any variable after week one
Training stress must gradually increase via load, volume, density, or complexity over time
Only flexibility can be overloaded, never strength
Overload means training through sharp joint pain

explanation: Adaptation requires progressive challenge within recovery capacity—not pain chasing.

distractorExplanations.c: All fitness qualities can progress.
distractorExplanations.d: Pain is a warning, not a method.

prompt: For a client with the goal of fat loss, resistance training is valuable because it:

options.text: Replaces the need for any dietary awareness forever
Guarantees spot reduction of belly fat only where electrodes are placed
Helps preserve lean mass and supports metabolic rate while in a calorie deficit
Makes cardiovascular exercise useless in all cases

explanation: Resistance training protects muscle during weight loss; nutrition and cardio still matter.

distractorExplanations.a: Energy balance still drives fat loss.
distractorExplanations.b: Spot reduction is a myth.
distractorExplanations.d: Cardio can still support energy expenditure and health.

prompt: Which breathing cue is generally appropriate during a heavy concentric lift?

options.text: Hyperventilate rapidly throughout every rep with no brace
Never breathe during any set
Only breathe during the warm-up, not working sets
Brace and exhale through the sticking point (avoid prolonged breath-holding if contraindicated)

explanation: Bracing with controlled breathing manages intra-abdominal pressure; Valsalva may be used carefully in advanced lifters without contraindications.

distractorExplanations.b: Breathing is required.
distractorExplanations.c: Working sets especially need bracing/breath strategy.

prompt: Integrated training in NASM refers to:

options.text: Combining flexibility, core, balance, SAQ, plyometric, and resistance training in a purposeful program
Only foam rolling forever
Only sport practice with no physical preparation
Only machine circuit training without any other qualities

explanation: OPT integrates multiple components rather than isolating one modality indefinitely.

distractorExplanations.b: SMR is one tool, not the whole program.
distractorExplanations.c: Sport skill alone is incomplete preparation.
distractorExplanations.d: Machines can be useful but are not the entire model.

prompt: A deload week is best described as:

options.text: A week of maximal testing every day
A planned reduction in volume and/or intensity to allow recovery and adaptation
Complete retirement from exercise permanently
Eating zero protein

explanation: Deloads manage fatigue while maintaining some training stimulus.

distractorExplanations.c: Deload is temporary.
distractorExplanations.d: Nutrition support remains important.

prompt: Which balance progression is most advanced?

options.text: Seated supported bilateral stance on a stable chair
Two-foot standing on a firm floor with eyes open and hand support
Single-leg unstable surface with perturbation / reactive tasks
Lying completely supported with no upright challenge

explanation: Progress by reducing base of support, adding instability, vision challenges, and reactions.

distractorExplanations.b: Bilateral firm stance is basic.
distractorExplanations.d: Lying removes balance demand.

prompt: Within typical CPT scope of practice, which nutrition action is appropriate?

options.text: Provide general healthy eating guidelines and refer to an RD for medical nutrition therapy
Prescribe a therapeutic ketogenic protocol for epilepsy as clinical treatment
Diagnose celiac disease from a food log
Tell a client to stop physician-prescribed medication to lose weight

explanation: CPTs educate with general guidelines; disease-specific MNT and diagnosis are for licensed clinicians.

distractorExplanations.b: Therapeutic diets for disease are RD/MD territory.
distractorExplanations.c: Diagnosis is medical.
distractorExplanations.d: Medication changes require the prescribing clinician.

prompt: Which macronutrient is the primary fuel for high-intensity anaerobic glycolysis?

options.text: Only dietary cholesterol
Carbohydrate
Only alcohol
Only insoluble fiber as ATP

explanation: Glycolysis uses carbohydrate (glucose/glycogen); fat is slower oxidative fuel; fiber is not a rapid ATP source.

distractorExplanations.a: Cholesterol is not a workout fuel.
distractorExplanations.c: Alcohol is not a training macronutrient strategy.
distractorExplanations.d: Fiber is poorly used for rapid ATP.

prompt: Recommended dietary protein for many exercising adults often falls near:

options.text: 0 g/day if lifting
10 g/kg/day for all sedentary adults as a universal rule
About 1.2–2.2 g/kg/day depending on goals and total energy intake (general fitness guidance ranges)
Protein has no role in muscle repair

explanation: Active individuals usually need more than sedentary RDAs; extreme values are unnecessary or unrealistic.

distractorExplanations.b: 10 g/kg is excessive for most people.
distractorExplanations.d: Protein supports repair and adaptation.

prompt: Which behavior-change technique helps clients turn goals into action?

options.text: Vague wishes without tracking
Public shaming as the only tool
Avoiding all feedback
Specific, measurable action plans and self-monitoring

explanation: Implementation intentions, tracking, and feedback support adherence.

distractorExplanations.b: Shame harms rapport and adherence.
distractorExplanations.c: Feedback guides adjustment.

prompt: A CPT should maintain client confidentiality except when:

options.text: Disclosure is required by law / duty-to-warn situations or the client provides appropriate consent
Gossiping with other gym members for fun
Posting client before/after photos without consent
Sharing private health details to market services without permission

explanation: Privacy is an ethical/legal duty; limited exceptions exist for legal obligations and consent.

distractorExplanations.b: Casual gossip is a breach.
distractorExplanations.c: Photos need consent.
distractorExplanations.d: Marketing use needs permission.

prompt: Which statement about dietary supplements is most accurate for a CPT?

options.text: Guarantee that any supplement sold in a gym cures disease
Discuss general evidence cautiously and refer clients to qualified professionals; do not prescribe like a clinician
Inject clients with peptides in the locker room
Claim FDA approval for all sports supplements automatically

explanation: Supplement advice has legal/ethical limits; disease claims and invasive procedures are out of scope.

distractorExplanations.a: Cure claims are inappropriate and often illegal.
distractorExplanations.c: Injections are not CPT practice.
distractorExplanations.d: Most supplements are not FDA-approved drugs.

prompt: SMART goals are:

options.text: Secret, Magical, Abstract, Random, Timeless
Only financial goals for the trainer
Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Realistic/Relevant, Time-bound
Goals that must never be written down

explanation: SMART structure improves clarity and accountability for client outcomes.

distractorExplanations.b: Client outcome goals matter, not only trainer revenue.
distractorExplanations.d: Writing goals supports tracking.

prompt: Which business practice is most professional?

options.text: Guaranteeing a 20-lb fat loss in 7 days for every client
Training minors without required consents/policies
Using another trainer’s credentials on your ads
Clear contracts, transparent pricing, documented scope of practice, and accurate marketing claims

explanation: Professional practice requires honesty, consent, and legal compliance.

distractorExplanations.b: Minors require proper consent/policies.
distractorExplanations.c: Credential misrepresentation is fraud.

prompt: Motivational interviewing emphasizes:

options.text: Collaborative, client-centered exploration of ambivalence and intrinsic motivation
Lecturing clients until they comply
Ignoring the client’s values
Threatening to cancel sessions for any slip

explanation: MI uses empathy, evocative questions, and partnership rather than confrontation alone.

distractorExplanations.b: Pure lecturing often increases resistance.
distractorExplanations.c: Values drive lasting change.
distractorExplanations.d: Threats damage autonomy and rapport.

prompt: Dehydration during training can impair performance by:

options.text: Improving sweat rate indefinitely with no downside
Reducing plasma volume, increasing cardiovascular strain, and impairing thermoregulation
Eliminating the need for electrolytes in all ultra-endurance events automatically
Increasing bone length acutely

explanation: Fluid deficits raise heart rate for a given workload and raise heat illness risk.

distractorExplanations.c: Long events may still need electrolyte strategies.
distractorExplanations.d: Hydration does not lengthen bones.

prompt: Which is an example of an open-ended coaching question?

options.text: Did you work out? (yes/no only, with no follow-up)
You failed again, right?
What barriers got in the way of your workouts this week?
Stop talking and just do what I say.

explanation: Open questions invite reflection and problem-solving; closed questions limit dialogue.

distractorExplanations.a: Yes/no questions can start, but open follow-ups deepen change talk.
distractorExplanations.b: Judgmental framing harms rapport.
distractorExplanations.d: Command-only coaching reduces autonomy.

prompt: A client asks for a meal plan to treat Type 2 diabetes. The CPT should:

options.text: Create a disease-treatment diet and adjust insulin doses
Tell them to stop metformin immediately
Diagnose the severity of retinopathy
Refer to a registered dietitian / physician for medical nutrition therapy and stay within general guidelines

explanation: Diabetes MNT and medication management require licensed clinicians.

distractorExplanations.b: Medication changes need the physician.
distractorExplanations.c: Eye disease diagnosis is medical.

prompt: Which recovery recommendation is generally evidence-aligned?

options.text: Prioritize sleep, progressive programming, and adequate nutrition/hydration
Sleep is optional if caffeine is high enough forever
Never take rest days regardless of fatigue and pain
Only passive ice baths replace all training recovery needs

explanation: Sleep and periodization are foundational; modalities are adjuncts.

distractorExplanations.b: Chronic sleep loss impairs adaptation.
distractorExplanations.c: Rest/recovery days manage load.
distractorExplanations.d: Cold therapy does not replace programming and sleep.

prompt: Liability risk is reduced when a CPT:

options.text: Ignores red-flag symptoms to keep billing high
Screens clients, documents sessions, uses informed consent, and stays in scope
Falsifies CPR/AED certification dates
Trains in prohibited medical procedures

explanation: Risk management combines screening, documentation, competence, and referral.

distractorExplanations.c: False credentials are illegal/unethical.
distractorExplanations.d: Out-of-scope acts increase liability.

prompt: Which statement about alcohol and fitness goals is most accurate?

options.text: Alcohol is an essential macronutrient required daily for hypertrophy
Alcohol always increases protein synthesis
Excessive intake can impair recovery, sleep, and calorie control
Alcohol hydration fully replaces water needs during training

explanation: Alcohol can displace nutrients, disrupt sleep, and add energy without supporting training adaptations.

distractorExplanations.a: Alcohol is not essential for muscle growth.
distractorExplanations.b: It can impair muscle protein synthesis/recovery.
distractorExplanations.d: It is not a hydration strategy for training.
